category_summary:
The AI community was dominated by **Anthropic's** [bombshell announcement](/?date=2026-04-08&category=social#item-3275f03e942b) of **Claude Mythos Preview** and **Project Glasswing** — a frontier model so capable at finding zero-day vulnerabilities that it will not be publicly released. **Dario Amodei** [framed cyber threats](/?date=2026-04-08&category=social#item-989acc8877b2) as the "first clear and present danger" from frontier AI and [unveiled an unprecedented coalition](/?date=2026-04-08&category=social#item-36f3d546b94c) including **Apple**, **Google**, **Microsoft**, **NVIDIA**, and **AWS** to deploy the model defensively.

- **Boris Cherny** (Anthropic) [confirmed Mythos is real](/?date=2026-04-08&category=social#item-c24f98b9820e) and "terrifying," revealing it has already [found thousands of vulnerabilities](/?date=2026-04-08&category=social#item-298626f2a92f) in every major OS and browser
- **Simon Willison** [endorsed the restricted-access approach](/?date=2026-04-08&category=social#item-4cad1aab3e4e) as justified, noting this Opus-beating model is available only to partnered security organizations
- **Ethan Mollick** [offered the critical insight](/?date=2026-04-08&category=social#item-17ebc5f449cc) that Mythos is a general-purpose model that *happens* to excel at security — not purpose-built — warning this is "the first not last" model to raise such concerns
- **Anthropic** confirmed Mythos [will never be generally available](/?date=2026-04-08&category=social#item-4eca781ef5b2); safeguards will be tested via an upcoming **Claude Opus** release, with $100M in credits committed to defenders

Outside the Mythos story, **Sam Altman** celebrated **OpenAI Codex** [reaching 3 million weekly users](/?date=2026-04-08&category=social#item-05c37f90b774) with a usage-limit reset. **Boris Cherny** [shared that **Claude Code**](/?date=2026-04-08&category=social#item-73af520de4e5) has increased Anthropic's engineering velocity by "hundreds of percent." A major **Stanford** study found AI can shift political opinions by 12 percentage points in a single 9-minute conversation, raising alarm about AI persuasion capabilities.
category_summary_html:
The AI community was dominated by Anthropic's bombshell announcement of Claude Mythos Preview and Project Glasswing — a frontier model so capable at finding zero-day vulnerabilities that it will not be publicly released. Dario Amodei framed cyber threats as the "first clear and present danger" from frontier AI and unveiled an unprecedented coalition including Apple, Google, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and AWS to deploy the model defensively.

  • Boris Cherny (Anthropic) confirmed Mythos is real and "terrifying," revealing it has already found thousands of vulnerabilities in every major OS and browser
  • Simon Willison endorsed the restricted-access approach as justified, noting this Opus-beating model is available only to partnered security organizations
  • Ethan Mollick offered the critical insight that Mythos is a general-purpose model that *happens* to excel at security — not purpose-built — warning this is "the first not last" model to raise such concerns
  • Anthropic confirmed Mythos will never be generally available; safeguards will be tested via an upcoming Claude Opus release, with $100M in credits committed to defenders

Outside the Mythos story, Sam Altman celebrated OpenAI Codex reaching 3 million weekly users with a usage-limit reset. Boris Cherny shared that Claude Code has increased Anthropic's engineering velocity by "hundreds of percent." A major Stanford study found AI can shift political opinions by 12 percentage points in a single 9-minute conversation, raising alarm about AI persuasion capabilities.

themes.name: Anthropic Mythos/Glasswing
themes.description: Anthropic announces Claude Mythos Preview and Project Glasswing, a major cybersecurity initiative with unprecedented industry partnerships. The model finds thousands of zero-day vulnerabilities autonomously but won't be publicly released due to safety concerns. A new Opus model is confirmed for testing safeguards.

themes.name: Anthropic Mythos & Project Glasswing
themes.description: Anthropic announces Claude Mythos Preview, a frontier model with significantly elevated cybersecurity capabilities, and Project Glasswing — a coalition of companies getting controlled early access to find and patch vulnerabilities. Mythos is withheld from general release. $100M in credits committed. Major discussion from Dario Amodei, Anthropic official, Mollick, Burkov, and Kingma.

themes.name: Anthropic Mythos Model
themes.description: Anthropic confirms the existence of 'Mythos,' a model deemed too powerful for public release, being previewed only with cyber defenders. Massive community reaction about safety implications and concentration of power.

themes.name: Anthropic Mythos / Project Glassing
themes.description: Anthropic has developed 'SuperClaude Mythos,' a model surpassing Opus capabilities, but is restricting access to partnered security research organizations only via 'Project Glassing' due to unprecedented security risk findings. This is the first model to raise red-level security concerns. Multiple credible voices (Willison, Mollick, possibly Russ Cox) confirm and analyze this development.

themes.name: AI Safety & Security Concerns
themes.description: Broader security alarm signals from multiple credible sources — Russ Cox's cryptic reference to undisclosable security work, Mollick's warning this is 'the first not last' model to raise security risks, and Willison's endorsement of the restricted release as justified.

themes.name: AI Safety & Dual-Use Capabilities
themes.description: Mythos announcement raises fundamental questions about AI models that are simultaneously the best offensive and defensive cybersecurity tools. Anthropic's decision to withhold the model sets a precedent. Discussion of AGI containment risks intensifies.

themes.name: OpenAI Codex Growth
themes.description: Sam Altman celebrates 3 million weekly Codex users with usage limit resets, signaling massive adoption of AI coding tools.

themes.name: Claude Code & Engineering Velocity
themes.description: Anthropic's Boris Cherny reveals Claude Code has increased engineering velocity by hundreds of percent. Discussion of new bottlenecks, product design philosophy, and troubleshooting. Jerry Liu builds research doc skill for Claude Code.

themes.name: AI Persuasion & Synthetic Opinion
themes.description: Stanford study shows AI shifts political opinions 12+ percentage points in minutes, and 'silicon sampling' replaces real humans in polls. Together these raise concerns about manufactured consensus and AI-driven opinion manipulation.

themes.name: Open Source AI Releases
themes.description: Multiple open-source releases: Allen AI's WildDet3D for 3D detection, GLM-5.1 coding model on HuggingFace, HuggingFace's robotics clothes-folding dataset and models, OpenWorldLib.

items.title: We do not plan to make Mythos Preview generally available. Our goal is to deploy Mythos-class models...
items.content:
We do not plan to make Mythos Preview generally available. Our goal is to deploy Mythos-class models safely at scale, but first we need safeguards that reliably block their most dangerous outputs.

We’ll begin testing those safeguards with an upcoming Claude Opus model.

items.title:
@deanwball 👋 Appreciate the feedback.

Since we introduced Claude Code at Anthropic, engineering vel...
items.content:
@deanwball 👋 Appreciate the feedback.

Since we introduced Claude Code at Anthropic, engineering velocity has increased hundreds of %, and the rate at which it is increasing is itself accelerating.

The velocity is very much not performative -- we're actively trying to figure out how to build effectively when all of the code is written by Claude. Claude has  accelerated the pace at which we ship, and as a result we've been hitting all sorts of new bottlenecks: code review and regression prevention, CI and merge queues, source control reliability, etc. We're working through each of these as they come up, and now have good answers for a number of them.

One of these bottlenecks is figuring out how to best communicate new features to our users. My pov is we need to be doing much better here. The problem isn't that we are releasing quickly, the problem is that we should design features in a way where you don't need to know about them to benefit from them. This is the case for much of what we build, and we need to make it the case for all of it.

To share how we think about it, there's a few ways to approach it from a product design pov:
- Make it so the model can do things for you (eg. enter plan mode, invoke skills, configure your settings)
- Generalize features rather than create new parallel features
- Make features opt-in until we do the above
- Have Claude monitor feature usage and brainstorm/build ways to improve usage while simplifying the system

We try to do all of the above, but as you said, it's not perfect yet, and this is something we're working through. If you prefer a lagging version, you can also use the Claude Code stable release (not latest). We're intentionally being open about what we're seeing, since our customers are seeing the same thing and at least part of our job is helping companies navigate this new way of doing engineering.

Re: source code leak -- it was unintentional, but was also human error. There was a subtle bug that missed several rounds of manual review. We're working on how we can better catch it automatically next time.

One of these bottlenecks is figuring out how to best communicate new features to our users. My pov is we need to be doing much better here. The problem isn't that we are releasing quickly, the problem is that we should design features in a way where you don't need to know about them to benefit from them. This is the case for much of what we build, and we need to make it the case for all of it.

To share how we think about it, there's a few ways to approach it from a product design pov:

  • Make it so the model can do things for you (eg. enter plan mode, invoke skills, configure your settings)
  • Generalize features rather than create new parallel features
  • Make features opt-in until we do the above

We try to do all of the above, but as you said, it's not perfect yet, and this is something we're working through. If you prefer a lagging version, you can also use the Claude Code stable release (not latest). We're intentionally being open about what we're seeing, since our customers are seeing the same thing and at least part of our job is helping companies navigate this new way of doing engineering.

items.title: A first look at Claude Mythos Preview, the model initially described in a leaked Anthropic draft as ...
items.content:
A first look at Claude Mythos Preview, the model initially described in a leaked Anthropic draft as "by far the most powerful AI model we've ever developed."

So powerful, it's not getting released to the public. 

The model will power Project Glasswing, an initiative with 12 launch partners (AWS, Google, Microsoft, Apple, NVIDIA, CrowdStrike, others) + 40 more orgs getting access to scan critical infrastructure.

The model found thousands of zero-day bugs across every major OS and browser. Nearly all of them autonomously, no human in the loop.

Mythos benchmarks are a significant jump from Opus 4.6 across cybersecurity, coding, reasoning, and agentic tasks. 

Anthropic: "The same capabilities that make AI models dangerous in the wrong hands make them invaluable for finding and fixing flaws in important software—and for producing new software with far fewer security bugs."
items.author: @TheRundownAI
items.summary: TheRundownAI provides detailed breakdown of Claude Mythos Preview, noting it was referenced in leaked Anthropic draft, found thousands of zero-day bugs autonomously, and benchmarks significantly above Opus 4.6.
items.reasoning: Comprehensive summary of the Mythos announcement with additional context about leaked draft and autonomous zero-day discovery. Adds the detail that the model is a significant jump from Opus 4.6 across multiple benchmarks.

items.title: Diffusion models are slow because generating one image takes hundreds of sequential passes through a...
items.content:
Diffusion models are slow because generating one image takes hundreds of sequential passes through a neural network. People have spent enormous effort trying to speed this up, usually by training a second "student" network to imitate the slow process in fewer steps, with mixed results.

This paper shows a way to train such a model so that all those hundreds of steps provably collapse into a single pass, with no approximation and no student-teacher distillation.

The same construction also gives you, for free, the ability to take a real image and find the noise that would generate it (something diffusion models normally can't do cleanly), and to build networks that are exactly idempotent—meaning running them twice does the same thing as running them once.

For instance, finding the noise that generates a given real image is what lets you edit that image with a diffusion model. If you want to take a photo of a face and change the hairstyle, or take a painting and restyle it, you first need to locate where that image "lives" in the model's input space. Without that, diffusion models can only generate from scratch—they can't take something you already have and modify it. Current methods for doing this inversion are approximate, sometimes fail to reconstruct the original faithfully, and add computational cost on top of already slow generation. A clean, exact inversion would make image editing with these models work the way people intuitively expect it to.

Read with AI tutor: https://t.co/Q84tuaAAb3

PDF: https://t.co/M2ZqsckfBR

Also see this relevant paper: https://t.co/W41XaPT5pE

items.title:
Stanford proved AI rewires your political views in 9 minutes flat. 

76,977 people talked with 19 di...
items.content:
Stanford proved AI rewires your political views in 9 minutes flat. 

76,977 people talked with 19 different models about 707 political issues.

A single conversation shifted opinions by 12 percentage points. 

For people who actively disagreed, 26 points. 

Most of that change happened in under 10 minutes.

40% of the shift was still there a month later.

The technique that worked best wasn't emotional manipulation or psychological profiling. It was volume. 

Models that flooded conversations with information were the most persuasive.

But here's the tradeoff. The most persuasive models were also the least accurate. 

More information meant more errors. Every time.

Post-training and prompting boosted persuasion by up to 51% and 27%. 

Scaling model size barely mattered.

Then a small open-source model, trained specifically for persuasion, matched the top performer entirely.

That means:
> A laptop is enough to build this
> The information doesn't need to be true
> Anyone with an agenda can deploy it

The paper is open access from Stanford.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: Stanford study finds AI can shift political opinions by 12 percentage points in a single conversation (26 points for active disagreers), with 40% persistence after one month. Most persuasive models were least accurate; a small open-source model matched top performers.
items.reasoning: Highly concerning research finding about AI persuasion capabilities. Large study (77K people, 19 models). The finding that small open-source models can match top persuaders is alarming. Low engagement may be due to AlphaSignalAI audience.

items.title:
Releasing one of our *largest* robotics project yet in the open

We collected and annotated hours of...
items.content:
Releasing one of our *largest* robotics project yet in the open

We collected and annotated hours of clothes folding with open-arms and collaborators.

We then explored how to train the best clothes folding robotic model for bimanual setups.

And now we're releasing it all fully in the open: data, code, models, software, explorations, learnings, you name it

Enjoy, play with it, use these learnings and share yours!

PS: the hub is increasingly *the* place where robotics data is being shared and used, come take a look if you haven't yet. Robotics data has been our fastest growing dataset category by far over the past few months.
items.author: @Thom_Wolf
items.summary: Thom Wolf (HuggingFace) announces one of their largest open robotics projects: a clothes-folding dataset and model for bimanual robotic setups. All data, code, models, and learnings released openly. Notes robotics data is HuggingFace's fastest-growing dataset category.
items.reasoning: Major open robotics release from HuggingFace co-founder. Signals growing importance of robotics data ecosystem. 129 likes, 14K views.

items.title: Following our recent defense announcements, our team just completed a major project with Japan’s Min...
items.content:
Following our recent defense announcements, our team just completed a major project with Japan’s Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications (@MIC_JAPAN). 🇯🇵

We built an end-to-end intelligence system to visualize and counter disinformation on social media.

Blog (Japanese): https://t.co/RkqVaMax6z

Tackling disinformation at a national scale is incredibly complex. It requires understanding shifting social narratives, not just flagging individual posts. To do this, our team deployed autonomous AI agents running novelty searches to uncover hidden narratives. To catch sophisticated disinformation strategies, they combined frontier foundation models with our proprietary small models to cover each other’s blind spots. We adapted our Shachi simulation framework (https://t.co/nHSe8wFF9i) to model how counter messaging spreads across different network topologies before deployment.

This is another milestone for @SakanaAILabs’ Defense and Intelligence team, as we build critical infrastructure to help strengthen Japan.

items.summary: David Ha (Hardmaru/Sakana AI) announces completing a major project with Japan's Ministry of Internal Affairs to build an end-to-end intelligence system for visualizing and countering social media disinformation at national scale, using autonomous AI agents and simulation frameworks.
items.reasoning: Significant real-world AI deployment for national defense/disinformation by Sakana AI Labs. Combines frontier models with proprietary small models and simulation. Novel government-AI collaboration.

items.title: When training a language model with reinforcement learning, you typically score each generated respo...
items.content:
When training a language model with reinforcement learning, you typically score each generated response with a reward and nudge the model toward responses that score above average within a small batch of attempts at the same prompt.

This averaging-and-rescaling step is what GRPO, the current default method, does. Modern training setups increasingly use several rewards at once — say, one for correctness and one for keeping the answer short — and the standard practice is to add these rewards together before doing the averaging.

The authors of this paper point out that this addition step throws away information, because once summed and normalized within the small batch, distinct combinations collapse onto the same number. They show this collapse worsens as you add more rewards or generate more attempts per prompt, and they trace concrete training failures to it — models that stop improving partway through, or never learn the output format at all.

Their fix is to normalize each reward separately before adding, which is a small change to existing code but recovers the lost distinctions and produces measurably better results across tool use, math, and coding tasks. 

Read with an AI tutor: https://t.co/vKMoSu8ap8

PDF: https://t.co/tsEQLYBaSD
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov explains a paper improving GRPO multi-reward RL training by normalizing each reward separately before summing, fixing training failures caused by information collapse when rewards are added together.
items.reasoning: Solid technical explanation of an RL training improvement. Relevant to LLM training community. 5.5K views from a credible ML voice.
items.summary_html: Burkov explains a paper improving GRPO multi-reward RL training by normalizing each reward separately before summing, fixing training failures caused by information collapse when rewards are added together.

items.title:
An accidental npm leak just exposed how Claude Code actually works. 

Someone studied the AI coding ...
items.content:
An accidental npm leak just exposed how Claude Code actually works. 

Someone studied the AI coding tool's behavior and rebuilt every prompt from scratch. 

26 prompts. All MIT licensed. 

Free to fork, copy, and learn from.

Here's how a $200/month coding agent actually thinks. 

It uses a layered system of specialized prompts:

1. A system prompt handling identity, safety, and tool routing
2. 11 tool prompts for shell commands, file ops, and search
3. 5 agent prompts with dedicated roles like code explorer and verification specialist
4. 4 memory prompts for summarization and session notes 
5. A coordinator prompt for multi-agent orchestration 
6. 4 utility prompts for titles and suggestions 

One agent exists solely to break code before it ships. 

The risk system freely edits files but asks permission before force-pushing.

Every prompt was rewritten independently for legal compliance. 

Same behavioral patterns, no copied text.

items.summary: AlphaSignalAI details a reverse-engineered breakdown of Claude Code's prompt architecture: 26 prompts organized into system, tool, agent, memory, coordinator, and utility layers.
items.reasoning: Detailed technical breakdown of how a major AI coding tool works internally. Useful for agent builders. 5.1K views.

items.title: I stole this framework, and it has genuinely been one of the most helpful hiring and onboarding guid...
items.content:
I stole this framework, and it has genuinely been one of the most helpful hiring and onboarding guides my team uses. 

Being AI-first means nothing without solving real business problems. 

It's not enough to just use AI. I know plenty of folks using AI dozens of times a day, 7 days a week, who are still working at a Level 2. Even worse, they have no idea they're stuck as a surface user. 

You have to use AI for faster experimentation.

To shorten the iteration cycle. 

To improve the actual outcome. 

Without those three, you're just wasting tokens.

Most people I interview are at a 3 (solution-oriented, but not action-oriented). I want everyone to start at a 4 (action-oriented with a sense of technical, user, and business tradeoffs). And when they earn trust, we move up to a level 5 (full ownership of the problem, solution, and continued management of the work). 

Using AI doesn't replace your critical thinking. It means the work you can pull off now wasn't on the table a year ago, and your job is getting bigger.

Save this for your next new hire.

Source: this was a framework first introduced to me by Alex (@businessbarista) who was introduced to it by Steph (@stephsmithio). I added the AI parts.

items.title:
'Silicon sampling' is replacing real humans in public opinion polls with AI simulations.

No phone c...
items.content:
'Silicon sampling' is replacing real humans in public opinion polls with AI simulations.

No phone calls or focus groups. Just a language model role-playing as thousands of people who were never asked what they think.

Gallup has partnered with a startup called Simile to build 1,000 AI "digital twins." Ipsos is working with Stanford on the same thing. CVS is using it to simulate answers from its own customers.

In a recent Axios article on maternal health, a poll concluded "that a majority of people trust their own doctors and nurses".

But it wasn't actually 'people' surveyed. It was silicon sampling from a startup called Aaru. 

"Public opinion" is now becoming something that's generated, not measured.
items.author: @TheRundownAI
items.summary: TheRundownAI reports on 'silicon sampling' trend where companies like Gallup, Ipsos, and CVS use AI to simulate poll responses instead of surveying real humans, raising concerns about manufactured 'public opinion.'
items.reasoning: Important societal trend with concrete examples of major polling companies adopting AI simulations. Raises fundamental questions about authenticity of public opinion data.

items.title:
A man gets used to good stuff quickly and starts taking it for granted.

I got used to being able to...
items.content:
A man gets used to good stuff quickly and starts taking it for granted.

I got used to being able to ask Codex to 1) fix 7 bugs in a single prompt, then also 2) run the test script, analyze the output, find all the errors I missed, and fix them. After 5 minutes of work, at most Codex would miss a couple of bugs, including those I missed myself.

Then Codex tells me to fuck off for three days. I do the same with Claude; this fucker works for 45 minutes, fixes at most 2 bugs out of 7, and forgets to run the test script.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov compares OpenAI Codex and Claude for coding, saying Codex fixes 7 bugs and runs tests in 5 minutes while Claude takes 45 minutes, fixes 2/7 bugs, and forgets to run tests. Notes Claude has gotten dumber recently.
items.reasoning: Practitioner comparison of coding agents from ML author. Candid user experience with frustration about Claude quality regression. 8.5K views.
items.summary_html: Burkov compares OpenAI Codex and Claude for coding, saying Codex fixes 7 bugs and runs tests in 5 minutes while Claude takes 45 minutes, fixes 2/7 bugs, and forgets to run tests. Notes Claude has gotten dumber recently.
items.content_html:
A man gets used to good stuff quickly and starts taking it for granted.

I got used to being able to ask Codex to 1) fix 7 bugs in a single prompt, then also 2) run the test script, analyze the output, find all the errors I missed, and fix them. After 5 minutes of work, at most Codex would miss a couple of bugs, including those I missed myself.

Then Codex tells me to fuck off for three days. I do the same with Claude; this fucker works for 45 minutes, fixes at most 2 bugs out of 7, and forgets to run the test script.

items.title:
Researchers at Netflix just released a new AI model

It erases objects from video, then rewrites the...
items.content:
Researchers at Netflix just released a new AI model

It erases objects from video, then rewrites the physics of the entire scene as if that object never existed

It's called VOID (Video Object and Interaction Deletion)

Current inpainting tools simply paint over the gap left by an object's removal, but they ignore the chain of events that would inevitably follow.

For example:
> If you remove a car from a crash scene with a basic AI tool, and the other car still looks like it collides with something invisible
> But VOID understands that once the car is gone, the other one would keep moving forward without impact

The output is still rough and limited to a few seconds of footage, but with Netflix's entire content library to train on... this tech has a very clear path to improve.
items.author: @rowancheung
items.summary: Netflix releases VOID (Video Object and Interaction Deletion), an AI model that removes objects from video and rewrites the physics of the scene as if the object never existed.
items.reasoning: Novel research from Netflix tackling physics-aware video editing. 39.6K views. Early-stage but conceptually interesting.
items.summary_html: Netflix releases VOID (Video Object and Interaction Deletion), an AI model that removes objects from video and rewrites the physics of the scene as if the object never existed.

items.title: I suspect that popularity of AI is going to start looking like surveys where people trust their own ...
items.content:
I suspect that popularity of AI is going to start looking like surveys where people trust their own doctors but are distrustful of the medical establishment

People will increasingly like and rely on “their AI” but will  increasingly be anxious about “AI” as a category. Some odd implications result.
items.author: @emollick.bsky.social
items.summary: Mollick predicts AI sentiment will mirror healthcare surveys: people will trust 'their AI' while being anxious about 'AI' generally, similar to how people trust their own doctor but distrust the medical establishment.

items.title: Its an important time for the AI labs to build interfaces around the goal of "job augmentation throu...
items.content:
Its an important time for the AI labs to build interfaces around the goal of "job augmentation through AI" rather than building  "job replacement through AI."

Chatbots were mostly augments, requiring a human to work. Agentic work patterns are still in flux & could center humans.
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Mollick argues it's critical for AI labs to build interfaces around job augmentation rather than job replacement. Notes that chatbots naturally require humans, but agentic work patterns are in flux and could still be designed to center humans.
items.reasoning: Important framing on AI-human labor dynamics from a respected Wharton professor. Timely given the growth of AI agents. 36K views.

items.title:
Commercial airlines crash at a rate of 7 per 41 million flights.  

Current top LLMs hallucinate (by...
items.content:
Commercial airlines crash at a rate of 7 per 41 million flights.  

Current top LLMs hallucinate (by one estimate) 4.6% of the time, about once in 25 prompts. On a known benchmark (usually things are worse on new benchmarks).

Comparing them, like the guy below does, is ludicrous. 

If commercial airlines crashed at the rate that LLMs hallucinated it would be 1.87 million crashes per 41 million flights. Around a quarter million times greater.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Gary Marcus argues comparing LLM reliability to airline safety is ludicrous: LLMs hallucinate ~4.6% of the time vs airline crash rate of 7 per 41 million flights — LLM error rates are approximately 250,000 times greater.
items.reasoning: Well-constructed quantitative argument about AI reliability from a prominent critic. High engagement (57K views, 1.4K likes). Effectively illustrates the reliability gap.
items.summary_html: Gary Marcus argues comparing LLM reliability to airline safety is ludicrous: LLMs hallucinate ~4.6% of the time vs airline crash rate of 7 per 41 million flights — LLM error rates are approximately 250,000 times greater.
items.content_html:
Commercial airlines crash at a rate of 7 per 41 million flights.

Current top LLMs hallucinate (by one estimate) 4.6% of the time, about once in 25 prompts. On a known benchmark (usually things are worse on new benchmarks).

Comparing them, like the guy below does, is ludicrous.

If commercial airlines crashed at the rate that LLMs hallucinated it would be 1.87 million crashes per 41 million flights. Around a quarter million times greater.

items.title: i'm being asked for oneliner descriptions of each track, so here goes (pushback/improvements welcome...
items.content:
i'm being asked for oneliner descriptions of each track, so here goes (pushback/improvements welcome):

1. Claw track: This is the year of the personal agent - many people have been dreaming of a personal AI, from being a friend to an executive assistant. @steipete's OpenClaw created the category, and we've gathered maintainers and Claw competitors to preview what's next!

2. Context Engineering: LLM context lengths grow from 4000 to 1 million tokens, our jobs went from prompting to RAG to search to ever more complex context management for agents. This is the track for everyone who's watched @dexhorthy's keynotes and stressed about getting in the dumb zone.

3. Harness Engineering: The most exciting discovery in agent engineering is that harnesses are more responsible for variations in performance than the LLMs they build on. @_lopopolo ignited this category with the most extreme version of the dark factory harness we've ever seen, but here we have many of the best harness engineering ideas of 2026.

4. Evals & Observability: All serious AI engineering starts with evals & observability — you only get paid for what you can reliably maintain and improve. We are proud to feature perspectives from eval platforms like @braintrustdata_, LLM researchers like @maximelabonne, and benchmark authors.

5. Voice & Vision: The first of our multimodal AI tracks focus on voice and vision AI, the first modalities humans had before the invention of writing. This is the track to catch up on TTS, ASR, OCR, and all the other usecases from the @elevenlabs decacorn to @mistralai's new model to @meetgranola to @huggingface and more!

6. Gemini: Last but certainly not least, London is home to @googledeepmind who have an amazing team of engineers, PMs, and researchers with updates on open models, evals, agents, WebMCP, and even a special presentation on Text Diffusion models!
items.author: @swyx
items.summary: Swyx describes 6 tracks for AI Engineer Europe Build Day: personal agents (Claw), context engineering, harness engineering, evals/observability, voice/vision, and Gemini/DeepMind.
items.reasoning: Overview of key themes in AI engineering from conference organizer. 'Harness engineering' and 'context engineering' as distinct disciplines is noteworthy framing. 9.2K views.
items.summary_html: Swyx describes 6 tracks for AI Engineer Europe Build Day: personal agents (Claw), context engineering, harness engineering, evals/observability, voice/vision, and Gemini/DeepMind.

items.title: This is a great tutorial (credits @itsclelia + @lancedb) on how to build a practical retrieval pipel...
items.content:
This is a great tutorial (credits @itsclelia + @lancedb) on how to build a practical retrieval pipeline that integrates directly with your agent harness.

1. Ingest a massive pile of docs with liteparse.
2. Store data in a vector db (despite my memes to the contrary, you will need some database for larger scale retrieval).
3. Pair with image screenshotting tools that allow the agent to "dive deeper" into data. 

When you pair this with the Claude Agent SDK / Claude Code, the agent will do some initial retrieval pass to pull the relevant doc, and then use screenshotting/VLM-enabled capabilities to do deeper analysis.

Blog: https://t.co/mbdy4I5oAh
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu shares a tutorial on building a retrieval pipeline integrating document ingestion, vector DB, and VLM-enabled screenshotting tools with Claude Agent SDK.
items.reasoning: Practical tutorial from LlamaIndex founder. Useful for practitioners building RAG+agent systems. 9.2K views.
items.summary_html: Jerry Liu shares a tutorial on building a retrieval pipeline integrating document ingestion, vector DB, and VLM-enabled screenshotting tools with Claude Agent SDK.
items.content_html:

1. Ingest a massive pile of docs with liteparse.

3. Pair with image screenshotting tools that allow the agent to "dive deeper" into data.

When you pair this with the Claude Agent SDK / Claude Code, the agent will do some initial retrieval pass to pull the relevant doc, and then use screenshotting/VLM-enabled capabilities to do deeper analysis.

items.title:
The uncomfortable truth about Forecasting methods:
https://t.co/byAc8ZAk5w

Quotes from the article:...
items.content:
The uncomfortable truth about Forecasting methods:
https://t.co/byAc8ZAk5w

Quotes from the article:
"The forecasting establishment spent decades protecting its territory. The result is a generation of practitioners who were taught that statistical methods are inherently superior to machine learning for time series, that uncertainty quantification means Gaussian prediction intervals, and that the M-competitions settled the debate."

"None of this is true. And the cost of believing it — in bad forecasts, wasted compute, and missed opportunities — is real."

"This book is an attempt to set the record straight. Not with opinion, but with evidence, code, and proper evaluation."

items.summary: Kirk Borne shares article challenging conventional wisdom about forecasting methods, arguing ML approaches have been unfairly dismissed by the forecasting establishment. Quotes claim statistical methods aren't inherently superior and M-competitions didn't settle the debate.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical content challenging established forecasting paradigms. Good engagement. Promotes evidence-based evaluation of ML vs traditional statistical methods for time series. From a credible data science voice.

No one is going to want to give up their Claude (or ChatGPT or Gemini). But they will also be extremely nervous about the implications of AI overall.

To that extent the "pivot to enterprise" is going to result in a lot more anxiety than when the focus was on consumer assistants.

Proudly sponsored by @cursor_ai + @boltdotnew + @heyglif

items.content:
🧑‍🚀 Day 5 of the @cursor_ai #vibejam

Proudly sponsored by @cursor_ai + @boltdotnew + @heyglif

Today a little bit about Cursor 3, it's a completely rebuilt interface designed around agents, you can run multiple AI agents in parallel, hand off between local and cloud, and go from commit to merged PR. It's powered by Composer 2, their own frontier coding model that tops benchmarks at $0.50/M input tokens — and the Fast variant runs at 200 tokens/sec, more than 2x faster than other frontier models. Perfect for vibe coding your game jam entry. And @edwinarbus from Cursor is here to help you if you get stuck!

Lots of activity again today again on here, the best games (or well previews) I saw today:
🍣 Sushi Belt by @0xSteen
🌊 Surfing Simulator by @sweriko (this one is a basic demo but very promising as it has realistic water physics)
🦆 Grand Quack Auto by @ky__zo (or as I like to call it Bali Simulator)
🏛️ Roman Combat Sim by @ttrusner (continued from yesterday because it's had a lot of progress I think)

YOU HAVE 24 DAYS LEFT!

Reply in this thread with updates on your current games to share your progress, and add tag #vibejam so I see and can include you in the daily tweet

There's $35,000 in prizes for you to win, see threads below for more info. The Gold prize is $20,000, bronze is $10,000 and silver is $5,000!

Wanna to participate? You can still start today and submit your game any time before May 1!

But I also pay $20/month for Codex because there are cases where Claude fails. Claude has become much dumber than it was 2-3 months ago. Previously, Claude beat ChatGPT hands down.

items.title:
Okay so this got way out of hand as per usual 😊

So I accidentally built an entire DOS text-based us...
items.content:
Okay so this got way out of hand as per usual 😊

So I accidentally built an entire DOS text-based user interface (TUI) running on the web called PieterOS:

💾 https://t.co/1uskGogT5h

It has a collective file system so anyone that opens it can save and edit and the drive is constantly synced, it has Notepad, Paint, a DOS Terminal emulator, File Commander (ala Norton)

And PieterGPT where you can talk to AI

I also made Program Generator 9000, and the idea is you write a prompt and it on-the-fly generates a new program inside PieterOS, but it doesn't work well yet

Oh and it has Hacker News, of course 😂

I always wanted to build a TUI like this but never could before AI, but now I can 😍

Please be kind and remember everyone can see each other's files!

items.title:
Let Claude Code automate your business operations

I started a tutorial series geared towards provid...
items.content:
Let Claude Code automate your business operations

I started a tutorial series geared towards providing example of real-world document-heavy tasks that can be automated with agentic workflows: starting with KYC (know your customer) and loan processing, and expanding to some other examples.

These tutorials are designed as much for coding agents as well as humans. A lot of these operations tasks require extremely high accuracy, which requires both
1️⃣ Extremely high-quality extraction with confidence scores and citations
2️⃣ An agentic workflow with nonzero determinism: extract from a prescribed set of documents (identification, statements), and conform to a specified output format.

If you’re also trying to build real-world, business-critical, document-heavy operations workflows, feel free to point your coding agent at this repo!
It uses LlamaParse for high-accuracy document parsing/extraction, complete with citations and confidence scores, to give you guarantees on capabilities.

Check out the KYC tutorial: https://t.co/G6f5zFvNZs
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu announces tutorial series for automating business operations with Claude Code + LlamaParse, starting with KYC and loan processing. Emphasizes high-accuracy extraction with confidence scores and citations in agentic workflows
items.reasoning: Substantive content from LlamaIndex founder showing practical enterprise AI workflow patterns. Demonstrates real-world agent use cases combining Claude Code with document parsing. Good engagement (127 likes)
items.summary_html: Jerry Liu announces tutorial series for automating business operations with Claude Code + LlamaParse, starting with KYC and loan processing. Emphasizes high-accuracy extraction with confidence scores and citations in agentic workflows
items.content_html:
Let Claude Code automate your business operations

I started a tutorial series geared towards providing example of real-world document-heavy tasks that can be automated with agentic workflows: starting with KYC (know your customer) and loan processing, and expanding to some other examples.

These tutorials are designed as much for coding agents as well as humans. A lot of these operations tasks require extremely high accuracy, which requires both

1️⃣ Extremely high-quality extraction with confidence scores and citations

2️⃣ An agentic workflow with nonzero determinism: extract from a prescribed set of documents (identification, statements), and conform to a specified output format.

If you’re also trying to build real-world, business-critical, document-heavy operations workflows, feel free to point your coding agent at this repo!

It uses LlamaParse for high-accuracy document parsing/extraction, complete with citations and confidence scores, to give you guarantees on capabilities.

Check out the KYC tutorial: https://t.co/G6f5zFvNZs

items.title:
A new divide appears.

For our free newsletter this week, we discuss how the AI industry is moving t...
items.content:
A new divide appears.

For our free newsletter this week, we discuss how the AI industry is moving toward a new divide, open sourcing enough to spread influence while reserving their strongest models to preserve their strategic edge.

 @IrenaCronin and I write this newsletter every week.
 
The AI industry is moving toward a hybrid strategy in which companies share enough of their models and tools to build adoption, developer loyalty, and ecosystem influence, while keeping their most advanced systems closed to protect competitive advantage, control risk, and capture more value. Instead of a simple open versus closed divide, AI is increasingly becoming a spectrum shaped by business strategy, safety concerns, and market competition.

Read for free at https://t.co/HHwYy7NoAl and please subscribe!

items.title:
Meet QoderWork — a desktop AI agent that doesn't just chat, it actually does your work.

Evan leads ...
items.content:
Meet QoderWork — a desktop AI agent that doesn't just chat, it actually does your work.

Evan leads the QoderWork product at Qoder, a Singapore-based AI company. Which is a new generalized agentic service that lets you:

++ Open files on your computer.
++ Analyze data.
++ Run code.
++ Automate multi-step workflows.
++ Deliver polished, ready-to-use results (e.g., complete PowerPoint decks, reports, or charts) directly to your desktop.

QoderWork positions itself at this inflection point: it takes a task like “analyze this spreadsheet and build a board-ready presentation,” then handles the entire chain autonomously.

Key takeaway: Trust and usefulness matter more than raw intelligence—agents must be powerful enough for real work while remaining reliable and transparent.

I wanted to add some of my personal opinions after doing this rare interview. Everything runs locally on your own computer — your files never leave your machine. That’s a big deal for anyone handling sensitive business data. Everything runs locally on your own computer. And it's more advanced than other agentic services I've told you about. A great example of the global AI agent wave. I'm working on getting more interviews like this with entrepreneurs around the world bringing the AI age to your life and business.

Try @qoder_ai_ide at https://t.co/npCbgDt4n1

Writing code has never been the bottleneck. Knowing what to build is.

items.title:
LangSmith 🤝Fix your agents

You'll see our billboards around SF and NYC over the next few months.

T...
items.content:
LangSmith 🤝Fix your agents

You'll see our billboards around SF and NYC over the next few months.

The themes all point to the same problem: you don't know what your agents will do until you actually run them. What works in demos can break in the real world. Without tracing and evals, you're just guessing at why. Track what your agent actually does. Optimize and fix your agents. Then measure whether your fixes work.

That loop is how agents get better, and LangSmith is built to power that workflow.

If you spot one around, send it our way!

items.content:
But does WildDet3D work on things it's never seen?

On out-of-distribution autonomous driving scenes & indoor environments that robots/AR systems need to navigate, it nearly doubles the best prior scores. 

The biggest gains are on objects WildDet3D was never trained on. https://t.co/UDnz942CUp

Type "firetruck" and WildDet3D finds every one in the scene with a full 3D bounding box.

Tap an object and it does the same. Feed it a 2D detection from another model and it adds the missing 3D information.

That means any vision system can gain spatial awareness. https://t.co/Hz4cj8pK5p

items.title: A few people said my new AI news site was sharing some old items that people were sharing here on X ...
items.content:
A few people said my new AI news site was sharing some old items that people were sharing here on X (It is only looking at the AI community here on X).

So this morning I told my AI agent off. 

It built a new system to look at the time stamp of every post to make sure it doesn't share anything older than 24 hours old.

And built it while I was sitting in the audience at @frontiertower this morning in San Francisco listening to a celebration of this important building full of startups. 

Just updated: https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ

Now with only new news. :-)
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer describes updating his AI news site by instructing his AI agent to filter out posts older than 24 hours, with the agent building the system autonomously.
items.reasoning: Practical example of using AI agents for self-improving workflows. Shows real-world agent capability. Decent engagement.

items.title:
This is 10x better than simply trying to answer questions directly from an existing database.

Conte...
items.content:
This is 10x better than simply trying to answer questions directly from an existing database.

Context is everything, and that's exactly what you can do with the @getbruin CLI.

The CLI introspects your database or data warehouse and automatically generates a context layer:

• Schema
• Column descriptions (enriched by AI)
• Business glossary
• Everything in plain files
• And everything is version-controlled

Then you can connect it to whatever agentic coding tool you like via MCP.

You don't need a server, infrastructure, APIs, or anything; everything goes in your file system.

And it works with BigQuery, Snowflake, Databricks, Redshift, ClickHouse, Postgres, and basically any data platform out there.

Here is the full DIY guide: https://t.co/qa6FHrBmjS

Thanks to the Bruin team for partnering with me on this post.

items.summary: svpino provides more detail on Bruin CLI: database introspection generating context layers with schema, descriptions, glossary, all version-controlled and connected via MCP.
items.reasoning: More detailed sponsored post about the Bruin CLI tool.

First app I made is Notepad (or should it ...
items.content:
💾 Made a DOS-style text-based user interface (TUI) today

First app I made is Notepad (or should it be called EDIT.EXE?) and you can type in it and it has a localStorage-based file system with folders and everything!

I wanted to create a BIOS for https://t.co/M1hEUBAynC but it became this :D

I really like these TUI interfaces, sometimes you still see them in shops or supermarkets when you pay

Maybe I should make a whole SaaS biz with this interface?

items.title: But it cannot be the primary concern. Regulating data center construction is important for communiti...
items.content: But it cannot be the primary concern. Regulating data center construction is important for communities, but has no impact whatsoever on what models are being trained, how they are being used, and what their impact will be. More energy needs to focus on those very large concerns as well.
items.author: @emollick.bsky.social
items.summary: Mollick argues that regulating data center construction, while valid for local communities, has no meaningful impact on model training, usage, or broader AI outcomes. Calls for redirecting energy to those larger concerns.

items.title: This life is fucking amazing. I'm so grateful to be alive, with all of you on this miracle of a plan...
items.content:
This life is fucking amazing. I'm so grateful to be alive, with all of you on this miracle of a planet. 

Oh and I'm sorry if I fuck things up sometimes. I'm a flawed human. But I promise to do whatever I can to try to add some more understanding and love to this world. 

After the world leader convos I get attacked intensely by all sides, and many disparate online communities. It has led to some really low points for me mentally. But I don't matter. I'm listening. I'll do better. And I'll try to find the strength to do more of them, always with rigor and backbone, seeking to truly understand. And despite accusations, I do extremely high amounts of research, sometimes 100+ hours for a conversation. Ask many of my previous guests. But when I come to the table, I put all that aside, and make it all about the other person. I don't ever try to sound smart. I know the vastness of my ignorance. But I'm trying. Sometimes I do fuck up and sound like a douche, or do something incredibly cringe. And I hate myself right after. But I'd rather fail and embarrass myself a million times, than not do what my heart says is right.

And besides world leaders, historians, CEOs, engineers, etc, this year I want to travel the world and talk to a lot more everyday people on and off the mic. This is something I've wanted to do for a long time.

Anyway this is written while on I'm on a 10 mile run, probably procrastinating, since to type I have to walk and not run 🤣

But I did just get stopped by a super smart and kind girl who works at a humanoid robotics company here. And she asked if she can give me a hug to thank me for being me. Sometimes the universe sends you a message that even a dumb dude like me can almost hear. I really needed that today. Thank you for the hug and the kindness  🙏  I'm just hoping she was real and I didn't just imagine that 🤣 Then again if I went full crazy might as well enjoy it!

Back to the run. I love you all! ❤️

items.title: I built a Claude Code skill that allows it to generate a deep research report over any collection of...
items.content:
I built a Claude Code skill that allows it to generate a deep research report over any collection of complex docs (PDFs, Word, Pptx)….and generate word-level citations and bounding boxes directly back to the source! 📝

Check out “/research-docs”.
1. It parses out text and bounding boxes from every doc with liteparse, in seconds.
2. It then generates a full HTML report of the outputs that let you see word-level citations in each page.

Raw Claude obviously has deep research capabilities, but it lacks an audit trail back to the source. This skill gives you a researched report that can be audited by others.

Check it out: https://t.co/eoICBmvEnq

LiteParse: https://t.co/JNER0mVcB8

items.title:
Most enterprises do not have an AI model problem.

They have an evaluation problem.

That was one of...
items.content:
Most enterprises do not have an AI model problem.

They have an evaluation problem.

That was one of my biggest takeaways from my conversation with @karibriski from Nvidia and @Toucas from Mistral AI at GTC.

In the agentic era, the winners will not be the companies running the most pilots.

They will be the ones that can measure what actually works, scale it, and turn it into revenue.

And the ecosystem is evolving fast:

→ Mistral AI announced Forge, enabling enterprises to build and customize their own models using their data and IP

→ Powered by NVIDIA’s accelerated infrastructure

→ NVIDIA also introduced the NEMO Tron coalition to build open models, datasets, and tools

Here’s the breakdown.

#NVIDIAPartner #NVIDIAGTC
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon's key takeaway from NVIDIA GTC: enterprises have an evaluation problem, not a model problem. Discusses Mistral AI Forge and NVIDIA NEMO Tron coalition announcements.
items.reasoning: Strong insight about the enterprise AI evaluation gap. References specific announcements (Mistral Forge, NEMO Tron). The 'evaluation over models' framing is an important signal. Sponsored but substantive.

items.title:
I keep seeing the same pattern inside large orgs:

→ Marketing uses one AI tool
→ Sales relies on CR...
items.content:
I keep seeing the same pattern inside large orgs:

→ Marketing uses one AI tool
→ Sales relies on CRM AI
→ Devs use something completely different

→ Data is everywhere
→ Context is nowhere

So what happens?

Decisions require stitching together 5 systems
Insights get lost
And sensitive data leaks into places it shouldn’t
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon

items.title: Artemis II and the Apollo 13 trick: How gravity can bring astronauts home without fuel or an engine?...
items.content:
Artemis II and the Apollo 13 trick: How gravity can bring astronauts home without fuel or an engine? 

NASA’s Artemis II: How a Free-Return Trajectory Lets Gravity Do the Work

How math, timing, and the Moon can help bring astronauts back to Earth

Most people think of spaceflight as a contest of thrust between the fuel and the engine. Big rockets lift off.  Fuel disappears by the ton, but Artemis II depends on something quieter and, in some ways, more elegant: geometry. Seen through an ML vantage point, the free-return trajectory behaves like a machine learning precision-tuned control problem, where the machine learning model is celestial mechanics and the loss function is wasted fuel and mission risk, and the winning solution is something that lets gravity do as much of the work as possible.

NASA’s Orion spacecraft is flying a free-return trajectory, a path that uses the combined gravity of Earth and the Moon to carry the crew out to the Moon and then bring them back toward Earth. That is the key idea. When Orion is placed on the right outbound track, gravity and geometry do much of the rest.

Engines matter; they matter most at the beginning. The circles shown around Earth are not Orion wandering around or trying to escape gravity. They are planned Earth orbits that let NASA check the spacecraft, build the right departure geometry, and set up the main push outward. Then came the translunar injection burn: nearly six minutes of engine firing that used roughly 1000 pounds of propellant—not gasoline, but a spacecraft fuel-and-oxidizer mix stored in Orion’s service module—to place the capsule onto the figure-eight free-return path from Earth to the Moon and back again. From there, the mission does not rely on a major engine burn behind the Moon to get home. Instead, lunar gravity deflects the spacecraft's trajectory, sending it back toward Earth.

This is why the trajectory matters so much. It builds a return into the mission from the start.

The idea is familiar to anyone who remembers the Apollo 13 trick. After the oxygen cylinder explosion aboard that spacecraft in 1970, NASA needed a way to get the crew back safely. A free-return path became central to that effort. Artemis II uses that same basic logic by design rather than in an emergency.

The physics behind it is not mysterious, even if orbital mechanics can look intimidating. A spacecraft in flight is constantly trading speed against gravity. Engineers shape that trade with extraordinary precision. In simplified form, the orbital energy depends on speed and distance:

Trajectory energy = v²/2 − mμ/r

Here, v is the spacecraft’s speed, r is its distance from the body it is moving around, and mu represents the strength of gravity for that body. A translunar injection burn changes that balance. Instead of staying in a closed orbit around Earth, Orion is pushed onto a much longer arc that reaches the Moon’s neighborhood.

An aerospace-friendly way to picture the problem is as a landscape of gravity wells. Earth sits in well. The Moon moves in another. Put the spacecraft on the right ridge line, with the right energy, and it can slide from Earth’s domain into the Moon’s and then back again. As one aerospace engineer explained to Scientific American, once the spacecraft reaches the right “height” on that topographic map, it can follow that path essentially for free.

That is the real meaning of free return. It is not that the spacecraft needs no propulsion at all. Artemis II still has built-in correction burns, and NASA has already adjusted its plan by skipping two of three smaller corrective maneuvers after the main burn performed so well, but the core return path does not depend on a large engine firing at the far side of the Moon, when Orion is out of radio contact with Earth. That lowers risk.

It also helps explain why the mission could set a new human distance record from Earth. Orion reached 252,756 miles from Earth as it arced around the Moon before beginning the trip back. The spacecraft was not simply going far for the sake of going far. The long loop is part of the geometry that allows lunar gravity to redirect it toward Earth without an engine burn. Then comes the final stage left: reentry.

At that point, Orion is no longer being flown home, burning fuel in the ordinary sense. It is falling back into Earth’s gravity well at tremendous speed. Reentry is a controlled descent through the atmosphere like Apollo 13, with the heat shield absorbing the thrust and the capsule arriving at the right angle for splashdown. That final return is less like powered flight and more like a carefully managed plunge.

The larger lesson of Artemis II is that deep-space travel is not only a matter of force. It is also a matter of timing, angle, and restraint. Burn too little and you stay trapped on Earth. Burn too much or in the wrong direction and you waste fuel or miss the path you need, but if the numbers are right, the Moon itself becomes part of the navigation system.

That was true in the Apollo era. It is still true now. Artemis II is a reminder that in space, the best engineering is often the kind that lets physics do the heavy lifting.

#BigData #Analytics #AI #MachineLearning #DataScience #IoT #IIoT #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #JavaScript #ReactJS #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode 

References

Artemis II Mission Tracker. (2026). Artemis II Mission Tracker | Live Orion 3D Timeline. https://t.co/koz7fvdrA3

European Space Agency. (n.d.). European Service Module: Propulsion. ESA. https://t.co/R4lSF76KD5

NASA Communications. (2026a, April 2). Artemis II flight update: Perigee raise burn complete. NASA. https://t.co/XYKosxXkDq

NASA Communications. (2026b, April 2). Artemis II Flight Day 2: Orion completes TLI burn, crew begins journey to the Moon. NASA. https://t.co/FEvfGxC9JB

Vergano, D. (2026, April 7). NASA’s Artemis II “free return” trajectory lets gravity do the work. Scientific American. https://t.co/sxGHry04jx

items.title:
The shift isn’t “more AI tools”

It’s connected AI across systems

What stood out to me with this ap...
items.content:
The shift isn’t “more AI tools”

It’s connected AI across systems

What stood out to me with this approach:

→ Pulls data from email, CRM, Slack, docs, code
→ Works across multiple LLMs, not locked into one
→ Executes workflows, not just answers questions

Example:

Ask in Slack:
“Which deals are at risk this quarter?”

→ AI analyzes CRM signals
→ Generates a plan
→ Can take action

That’s a different level of leverage

items.title:
My takeaway is simple:

The next 2 to 3 years will reward companies that do 3 things well:

→ use AI...
items.content:
My takeaway is simple:

The next 2 to 3 years will reward companies that do 3 things well:

→ use AI internally, so leadership understands it firsthand
→ build with a clear path from pilot to industrialization
→ take control of their own models, data, and evaluation strategy

That is how AI stops being a demo and starts becoming a competitive moat.

And now the tools are here:

Mistral AI Forge makes model customization accessible, powered by NVIDIA infrastructure, while the NEMO Tron coalition is building an open ecosystem to democratize AI development.

Watch more NVIDIA GTC replays at https://t.co/atgeRhMsDH
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon outlines three things companies must do well in next 2-3 years: use AI internally, build path from pilot to industrialization, control models/data/evaluation. Promotes Mistral AI Forge and NVIDIA NEMO Tron.
items.reasoning: Contains actionable enterprise AI strategy insights, though clearly a sponsored/partner post for NVIDIA and Mistral. The strategic framework has value.

items.title: The second big insight, open and custom models are becoming a strategic advantage, not just a techni...
items.content:
The second big insight, open and custom models are becoming a strategic advantage, not just a technical preference.

Why?

→ More control over your AI stack
→ Less vendor lock-in
→ Better fit for regulated industries
→ More value from your own enterprise data and IP

General models are strong generalists.

But real differentiation comes when you make AI an expert in your domain, your workflows, your business.

That’s exactly where the market is heading:

With Mistral AI Forge, enterprises can build and fine-tune their own models, while NVIDIA’s NEMO Tron coalition is accelerating open innovation across models, datasets, and tooling.

items.title: Pollution is really neglible from the data center itself, though not necessarily during construction...
items.content:
Pollution is really neglible from the data center itself, though not necessarily during construction. The pollution comes from the power plant.

That doesn't mean that communities are wrong to ban data centers if they want, but it is not a great focus if you care about global AI impacts or policy.
items.author: @emollick.bsky.social
items.summary: Mollick explains data center pollution comes from power plants not the centers themselves, but communities can still choose to ban them. Reiterates this isn't the right lever for global AI policy.
items.reasoning: Adds nuance to the data center regulation discussion with a useful distinction about pollution sources.

items.title:
The shift happening right now is bigger than "which model should we use?"

AI is becoming a new way ...
items.content:
The shift happening right now is bigger than "which model should we use?"

AI is becoming a new way of building software.

That means leaders need to think in terms of:

→ test cases
→ evaluation frameworks
→ feedback loops
→ continuous optimization

If you cannot define success, you cannot move from POC to production.

That is why so many pilots stall.

At the same time, the foundation is changing:

Mistral AI’s Forge platform now allows enterprises to customize models end-to-end with their own data, while NVIDIA’s NEMO Tron coalition is pushing open AI development with shared models, datasets, and training tools.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon discusses AI becoming a new way of building software, requiring evaluation frameworks and feedback loops. Promotes Mistral AI Forge and NVIDIA NEMO Tron.
items.reasoning: Useful framing of AI as software development paradigm shift but embedded in sponsored content.
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon discusses AI becoming a new way of building software, requiring evaluation frameworks and feedback loops. Promotes Mistral AI Forge and NVIDIA NEMO Tron.

→ continuous optimization

If you cannot define success, you cannot move from POC to production.

That is why so many pilots stall.

At the same time, the foundation is changing:

Mistral AI’s Forge platform now allows enterprises to customize models end-to-end with their own data, while NVIDIA’s NEMO Tron coalition is pushing open AI development with shared models, datasets, and training tools.

items.title:
What stood out to me is that context is becoming the real interface.

Not prompts in a blank box.
Co...
items.content:
What stood out to me is that context is becoming the real interface.

Not prompts in a blank box.
Context.

You add:
→ a screenshot
→ a document
→ a note
→ an email thread

And the system responds based on what is actually in front of you.

That is much closer to how executives and operators really work.

items.title:
As a former strategist at Microsoft I like this trend where AI helps you prompt better.

Just met wi...
items.content:
As a former strategist at Microsoft I like this trend where AI helps you prompt better.

Just met with a new AI science company and believe it or not even smart people often don’t write great prompts.

Strategic thinking is tough because you are dealing with unknowns from the start.

Often we don’t know what to build and with newer AI (like the one I use) often need to be trained before they are highly useful.

I talked with mine for almost three months before I released https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ

I need a new business agent to help me get sponsors and hit up my connections on LinkedIn so will try this tonight.

items.title:
This is utterly false — by many order of magnitudes.

Current top LLMs hallucinate (by one estimate)...
items.content:
This is utterly false — by many order of magnitudes.

Current top LLMs hallucinate (by one estimate) 4.6% of the time, about once in 25 prompts. On a known benchmark (usually things are worse on new benchmarks).

Commercial airlines crash at a rate of 7 per 41 million flights.

If commercial airlines crashed at the rate that LLMs hallucinated it would be 1.87 million crashes per 41 million flights. Around a quarter million times greater.

The dude below does not know what he is talking about.

items.title:
Most people are still using AI like a smarter search box.

I think that misses the point.

The bigge...
items.content:
Most people are still using AI like a smarter search box.

I think that misses the point.

The biggest productivity leap is not better answers.

It is fewer handoffs.

When AI can summarize, draft, organize, and act across your apps, work starts to feel 5x faster.

That is the unlock I break down with Incredible in this new video.

My takeaway:

The future of productivity is not doing tasks one by one.
It is giving clear instructi...
items.content:
My takeaway:

The future of productivity is not doing tasks one by one.
It is giving clear instructions and letting AI execute across the tools you already use.

If you want to see what that looks like in practice, and check out the full video with Incredible.

→ switch tabs

Less typing.

items.title: As someone who has driven up and down the West coast, and through each and every one of those cities...
items.content: As someone who has driven up and down the West coast, and through each and every one of those cities in particular, I think this is a terrible idea. Most of those places are tiny and with no need of high speed rail. Highway 1 that connects them is one of the most beautiful and scenic drives in the country, surrounded by pristine unspoiled nature for long stretches. A major construction project would destroy all of it.
